sop.coo_evaluation:
# The Forge SOP Assessment

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation** — no documented path for injuries, billing disputes, harassment, or facility issues; critical for liability and retention
- **Churn win-back campaign** — no SOP for identifying at-risk members, re-engagement triggers, or lapsed-member reactivation sequences
- **Coach performance management & underperformance** — no formal review cadence, feedback loops, or capability plan process
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** — no monthly bank/card reconciliation, refund approval workflow, or cash handling SOP
- **Health & safety incident log & corrective action** — no documented process for reporting, investigating, or resolving near-misses or accidents
- **New class launch & discontinuation** — no SOP for piloting, scheduling changes, or communicating schedule updates to members
- **Member data privacy & GDPR** — no documented process for consent, data requests, retention, or deletion
- **Staffing recruitment & onboarding beyond coaches** — no SOP for hiring reception, cleaning, or admin staff

**Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding (17/17)** — likely missing probation checkpoints, skill assessment milestones, or exit/offboarding playbook
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12 complete)** — Email Newsletter and Blog Post are incomplete; also missing member referral incentive mechanics and campaign approval workflows
- **Client onboarding (22/22)** — verify it covers induction assessments, goal-setting, first-class experience, and follow-up touchpoints; may lack upsell/package upgrade triggers
- **Facilities (4/4)** — likely missing deep-clean schedule, equipment maintenance log, or member access/key management process
- **Pricing & package SOP** — no clarity on when/how to

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## REVENUE TREND & ANALYSIS
**June vs May:** Revenue down 12.3% (£20,199 → £17,711). **Q2 (Apr–Jun) vs Q1 (Jan–Mar):** Revenue up 9.2% (£50,147 → £56,465), but profitability collapsed. This is driven by active client growth (94 → 147, +56%) that *isn't translating to profit*—membership quality or pricing is broken.

---

## 1. INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing audit:** Your Average Revenue per Member (AR/M) has collapsed from £170 (December peak) to £120 (June). With 147 members, you should be hitting £25,000+/month revenue at December rates. **Action:** segment your 147 members by tier, identify who's on discount/intro rates, and systematically move budget-tier members to standard pricing (even a £5–10/month uplift across 100 members = £500–1,000/month quick win).

- **Churn crisis in April (8.33%) just killed momentum.** You gained 2 net members June-on-June but lost pricing power. **Action:** run a 30-day retention sprint: 1-to-1 check-ins with the 147, identify why April was so leaky (likely new-member onboarding failure), implement a 90-day buddy system for joiners.

- **New revenue lines urgently needed:** one-off classes, PT sessions, merchandise, or corporate team packages. You're relying entirely on membership fees. **Action:** launch paid PT trial blocks (3 x £45 sessions) marketed to existing members; target 8 uptakes in July = £360 new revenue, ~5% margin lift.

---

## 2. REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people costs are the smoking gun.** May £9,233 → June £9,390, but April was £30,511 (anomaly or real?). Even at £9,400/month, that's 53% of revenue. **Against benchmark, facility costs should be 25–30%**. **Action:** itemise this bucket—utilities, rent, insurance, cleaning, equipment maintenance. One line item is likely overrun (rent rebill from landlord, emergency equipment repair, or supplier contract renegotiation missed). Audit and renegotiate.

- **Staffing is lean and stable** (£3,550 May, £3,686 June = 18–21% of revenue, acceptable). **However**, if you

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is significantly over-loaded at 133 sessions.** Redistribute her Personal Training clients (44 sessions) between Grace and Eilis to bring her closer to 100 sessions monthly. This protects against burnout and frees capacity for strategic priorities.

• **Grace is under-utilised at 52 sessions** despite being Founder & Head Coach. She should take on more PT clients from JoJo's caseload and lead the speciality programmes (Strongevity, No Sweat Intro) that only she runs—these are brand differentiators that need owner involvement.

• **Body Composition Scans are over-dependent on JoJo (20 of 40 total).** Train Eilis to conduct these independently so she reaches ~70–80 sessions; scans are a revenue and retention tool that should be distributed fairly across the team.

• **Build: Upper classes rely too heavily on JoJo (15 of 33 sessions).** Have Grace or Eilis co-lead or solo some of these recurring slots to create redundancy and give JoJo breathing room.

• **Elevate and Circuits have minimal coverage.** Confirm these classes have demand; if yes, assign Eilis to lead Circuits (plays to group-class strength) and ensure Elevate has a backup coach so Grace isn't sole lead.

• **Agree a monthly target of ~85–95 sessions per coach** and review PT allocation in July to balance workload fairly and sustainably.

class_stats.suggestion:
# Suggestions for The Forge

**Move low-attendance classes to off-peak times.** Schedule Engine, Capacity, and Barbelle on Monday or Thursday evenings when demand is lower, rather than competing with Build classes on peak days. This maximizes instructor utilization and may attract members who avoid busy sessions.

**Add extra Build: Upper and Build: Lower slots on Wednesday and Tuesday.** These are consistently the most popular classes and your busiest days—adding a second session during peak hours (likely lunch or early evening) will capture demand and prevent class caps limiting revenue.

**Consolidate or refresh underperforming classes.** Engine, Capacity, Barbelle, and Strongevity have dropped significantly in the last 30 days. Either rebrand/reposition them with new marketing, merge them into hybrid offerings, or redirect that timetable space to proven winners like Build classes.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Concrete Execute Path

• **Start with your revenue breakdown, not aspirations.** Pull last 3 months of actual sales data. What are members actually buying? (classes, PT, packages, add-ons). Build the menu around *what's working*, then layer in what you want to push. This kills the "pretty menu nobody uses" problem.

• **Price anchoring first, then packaging.** Set your highest-value service (likely 1-on-1 PT or transformation packages) at a number that makes you uncomfortable. Then work backward — membership tiers and class combos should sit 30-40% below that anchor to feel like "deals." This stops the race-to-bottom discounting trap.

• **Create two documents, not one.** Internal coach version = what they can sell, commission structure, upsell triggers (e.g., "if member hits plateau in week 6, pitch 4-week PT block"). Member version = benefits-focused (not features), outcome-driven copy. Coaches need permission and incentive; members need to see transformation, not just service names.

• **Run a 48-hour internal pilot.** Before launching externally, give coaches the new menu Friday morning. Capture what questions they ask, what feels unclear, what they won't confidently sell. Refine Monday before it touches members.

• **Embed it into your booking system THIS WEEK.** Don't just design it — integrate pricing, package rules, and upsell prompts into Mindbody (or whatever you use) so it's live, not theoretical. Live = behavioral change; document in a drawer = waste.

What's your current booking system?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Execution for The Forge

• **Audit your drop-off points first** — Pull analytics on WHERE people bounce (homepage, pricing page, class details?). Interview 5 recent sign-ups asking "what almost stopped you?" and 5 who didn't convert asking "what confused you?" This tells you what to actually fix, not what you think is broken.

• **Answer the ONE question holding women back** — Women-only gyms trigger specific concerns: "Will I be judged on my fitness level?" "Is this just cardio/weights/functional?" "How different is this from Planet Fitness?" Pick the #1 objection from your research above and make it the hero of your headline or first section.

• **Structure pages around her decision journey, not your services** — Instead of listing "spin classes, PT, nutrition coaching," map: *Why she came* (confidence, real results, community) → *Why The Forge specifically* (women-only removes intimidation, expert coaches, proven outcomes) → *No-risk entry point* (free session? 14-day trial?). Move FAQ higher—don't bury it.

• **Test and measure today** — Create two versions of your main funnel page (existing vs. refined). Split traffic 50/50 for 2 weeks. Track conversion rate + which CTA gets clicked most. You'll know what actually works.

• **Brief your designer with customer language, not your language** — Don't say "emphasize our holistic approach." Say "make it obvious we don't let women feel lost or out of place on day one."

What's your current conversion rate? That's your baseline to beat.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System for The Forge

**The Sharp Approach:**

• **Start with post-class capture, not forms** — Train instructors to ask 2 questions verbally during cool-down (takes 60 seconds): "What worked for you today?" + "One thing we could improve?" Record responses in a shared Google Sheet by class/instructor. Forms kill response rates; conversations drive honesty.

• **Make it an instructor accountability tool first** — Share compiled feedback *directly with each instructor* weekly, not just management. Instructors competing on positive feedback drives behavior change faster than top-down mandates. Highlight one win per instructor in your team chat.

• **Tie it to a visible member benefit within 4 weeks** — Pick the #1 complaint from month one and implement it visibly (e.g., better music selection, schedule change, new equipment). Announce: "We listened—here's what we fixed." This proves the system works and kills cynicism about feedback disappearing into a void.

• **Monthly 10-min huddle, not lengthy reports** — Share top 3 insights + top 3 wins with the team. Keep members in the loop via a simple Instagram Story: "This month you asked for X, we're doing Y." Transparency converts feedback participation into loyalty.

• **Automate the monthly ask** — Set a recurring calendar reminder for the 25th of each month to pull data and share insights. 15 minutes of admin beats a system that dies after month two.

What's your biggest current friction point with members?

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Start with your revenue-driver first: Personal Training**
Write the PT SOP *this week*. It's your highest-margin service and most operationally complex (scheduling, form corrections, progression tracking). Getting this locked down gives you a template and quick win. Use your top PT's actual workflow—observe them for 2 hours, document exactly what they do pre-session, during, and post-session (including how they upsell/retain).

**2. Batch the "simpler" ones: Membership + Fuel Forward**
These two have overlapping touchpoints (onboarding, billing, usage). Write them back-to-back in one session. For Membership SOP, focus on the *decision tree* (what happens when someone cancels vs. pauses vs. goes inactive). For Fuel Forward, document the exact ordering process, payment reconciliation, and reorder cadence—this prevents margin leaks.

**3. Build Well75 and Strongevity SOPs from your existing content/instructor notes**
These aren't new—you're just *systematizing* what's already happening. Have the instructors/coordinators send you their current notes/checklists, then structure them into: Pre-event checklist → During → Post-event (follow-up, feedback collection, upselling next tier). 30-minute phone call per program beats lengthy documentation requests.

**4. Use a dead-simple template for consistency**
Every SOP = [Trigger/When This Happens] → [Step-by-step Actions] → [Success Metrics] → [Common Issues + Fixes]. One page each, max. Store in a shared doc (Google Drive

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar Execution for The Forge

**Get it live THIS WEEK with this specific workflow:**

• **Use a free template, not a fancy tool yet** — Grab a Google Sheet or Notion template, create 4 columns: (Date | Post Type | Copy Hook | Visual Asset). Fill 8 weeks out on Sunday afternoons. This takes 90 mins max and kills the "what do I post?" paralysis that kills consistency.

• **Batch-create visuals in one 2-hour session** — Use Canva's women-focused fitness templates. Film 4-5 short gym clips in one day (transformation moments, class energy, member wins, form tips). This beats scrambling daily and keeps your aesthetic consistent. Store them in a dated folder.

• **Assign ONE person as "calendar keeper"** — Delegate to your most reliable staff member or invest in a VA for 3 hours/week ($40-60). Their job: pull from the calendar each morning, schedule via Buffer (free version works), add 2-3 community responses (comments/DMs). Without ownership, it dies.

• **Anchor 3 post types for predictability** — E.g., Monday = Member Spotlight (builds community), Wednesday = Training Tip, Friday = Class/Event Promo. Members start expecting it; you stop deciding what to post. Friction drops 80%.

• **Measure only what drives sign-ups** — Track clicks to booking page and DM inquiries, not likes. Review Sundays. If a format bombs, swap it next month. This keeps you honest and focused on revenue, not vanity metrics.

**Start this week. 90 minutes to template +

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Getting Videography Sessions Actually Done (Every 8 Weeks)

• **Lock it in NOW as a standing appointment** — not a "we should do this" conversation. Book Jordan for the same day/time slot every 8 weeks (e.g., first Tuesday of every other month). A recurring calendar invite with both of you beats any to-do list.

• **Create a 2-day content sprint around each session** — shoot 40-50% more than you need in one go. Film class snippets, member testimonials, equipment demos, transformation angles. One session = 8 weeks of Instagram/TikTok/website content. This justifies the cost and effort.

• **Define "the shot list" before Jordan arrives** — send him 5-7 specific clips you need (don't wing it). "Class energy from side angle," "new member onboarding," "free weights area walkthrough." He'll work faster and you'll get usable footage, not hours to sort through.

• **Budget £300-500 per session into your marketing spend** — stop treating videography as a luxury add-on. It's your conversion tool. Knowing the cost upfront means you'll actually use what you pay for.

• **Assign one team member as "content owner"** — they brief Jordan, wrangle members for testimonials, ensure lighting/setup is ready. Grace, this can't be you or it won't happen. Make it someone's responsibility.

What's your current biggest bottleneck with getting video content out — shooting it or actually publishing/using it?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Trial Enquiry Automations — The Forge

**The Real Problem:** Your trial sign-ups probably go into a spreadsheet or email folder, then require manual follow-up. You're losing conversions to inconsistency and forgetting sequences.

**Here's what to actually do:**

• **Map the 6-week journey first** — Document exactly what happens: Day 1 (welcome email + gym tour booking), Day 3 (check-in text if no tour booked), Day 14 (mid-trial motivation email), Day 35 (upgrade decision email), Day 40 (final push if silent). Write this down before touching any tools.

• **Use Typeform → Zapier → Google Sheets + your email tool** — When someone fills your trial form, Zapier automatically creates a row in Sheets AND triggers your first welcome email. You're not paying for fancy software; you're using what works. Set up 4-5 Zaps (one per touchpoint).

• **Segment by engagement in week 2** — After day 14 check-in, manually sort responders vs. ghosts into separate email lists. "Engaged" gets motivational content; "silent" gets a direct "We miss you" text from YOU personally. This 10-minute sort doubles conversions.

• **Test SMS over email for day 3** — Your members are women in the gym demo. A text "Hi [name]! Ready for that tour? Reply YES and pick a time →" gets 3x higher response than email. Automate via Typeform trigger.

• **Assign one person (you?) to handle day 35+ decisions** — Don't automate the upgrade

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist Execution for The Forge

**1. Role-Specific KPI Mapping (Do This First)**
Don't create generic checklists. Sit with each person individually for 30 mins and ask: "What 3-4 things, if you nailed them weekly, would directly grow revenue or reduce churn?" JoJo (assume sales/classes) owns new member conversions + class utilization %. Eilis (ops?) owns retention metrics + facility uptime. Sarah (assume programming/experience?) owns class feedback scores + retention in her cohorts. Write THEIR answers down, not yours.

**2. Make Checklists Brutally Specific & Measurable**
Instead of "increase sales activity," JoJo's checklist says: "5 trial bookings confirmed by Wednesday EOD. Conversion rate target: 40%+. Log in CRM daily." Not tasks—outcomes with success thresholds. Make each checklist fit on one A4 sheet so it's actually usable, not filed away.

**3. Build in a 10-Minute Weekly Review Ritual**
Every Monday morning (or Friday EOD), you review each person's checklist for 2-3 mins each. This isn't a performance interrogation—it's momentum building. Note wins, flag blockers, adjust KPIs if market conditions changed. Document the outcome in a shared doc (Google Sheet works fine) so accountability is visible.

**4. Tie Checklists to ONE Business Goal**
If Grace's priority is hitting 200 active members, make sure each person's KPIs ladder into that number. JoJo drives sign-ups, Eilis stops ch

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before you meet (do this NOW):**
- Create a **one-page visual** of your SSO (Significant Strategic Objective) + 90-day plan with clear milestones tied to revenue/membership targets—print it, don't PowerPoint it. People retain physical handouts better.
- Map each team member's name directly against specific 90-day deliverables (e.g., "Sarah: 15 new member onboardings by end of Q1" not "membership growth"). This removes ambiguity.

**During the session (60 mins max):**
- Start by stating the brutal reality: why this 90-day plan exists NOW (competitive pressure? revenue target? member retention dip?). Frame it as "here's why we're doing this, not just what we're doing."
- Go person-by-person and have *them* articulate back their lane in their own words—don't just tell them. If they get it wrong, you've got a teaching moment before they go execute.
- Define ONE success metric per person they'll report on weekly (not monthly). This keeps accountability real and catches problems early.

**The unmissable detail:**
- Ask: *"What's blocking you from crushing this?"* before they leave. Surface resource gaps, skill gaps, or conflicting priorities right then—don't discover this in week 4.

What's your current team size at The Forge?

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge

**Start with time-tracking reality, not assumptions**
Have each team member (trainers, desk staff, class instructors) log actual hours spent on tasks for ONE WEEK — not estimates. Use a simple shared spreadsheet: category, time spent, date. This reveals what's really eating time vs. what you *think* is.

**Map against revenue-generating vs. admin friction**
Plot each role's time by: direct client delivery (classes, PT), sales/retention, admin burden (cleaning schedules, booking systems, compliance). If someone's doing 4+ hours weekly on non-revenue tasks, that's your rebalancing lever — automate it or reassign.

**Identify the bottleneck person(s) immediately**
Usually one staff member becomes the "everything person" (scheduling, member issues, content creation). Name them explicitly in your audit. Then: what 3 tasks can you remove from them THIS WEEK? Even small wins reduce burnout.

**Redistribute with clear metrics, not guilt**
Don't just "redistribute fairly." Instead: "If Sarah takes over social content (5 hrs/week), Zara reduces her class load by one session." Make trades visible so people see the rebalancing logic — avoids resentment.

**Run a 2-week check-in, not 30-day wait**
After 10 days, grab 15 minutes with overtaxed staff. Reality check: Is the new load actually working? Small teams = quick pivots. Adjustments now beat waiting 30 days to discover it failed.

# Cutting 10% at The Forge — Concrete Moves

• **Audit your top 3 cost buckets first** (likely: staff, rent, equipment maintenance). Call your suppliers THIS WEEK with "I'm reviewing all contracts—what's your best rate if I commit for 12 months?" Even 3-5% wins on 2-3 categories gets you to 10% fast without touching service quality.

• **Reduce peak-hour staffing overlap by 15 mins** — analyse your actual busy periods (probably 6-8am, 5-7pm). Shift one staff member's clock in/out to cover the tail-end instead of full overlap. Sounds tiny, but it's real money without cutting presence when members are there.

• **Renegotiate your utility contract now** — fitness gyms spike energy costs. Contact your provider and ask for a small-business rate review. If you've been with them 12+ months, they'll often drop rates 8-12% to keep you. Takes one call.

• **Cut or consolidate your app/software stack** — most gym owners pay for overlapping tools (class booking + member app + payment processor + comms). You likely have 2-3 tools doing similar things. Consolidate into one platform = immediate 20-30% saving on that line item alone.

• **Stop over-ordering cleaning supplies** — switch to a par system (set minimum stock levels) instead of weekly auto-delivery. You're probably sitting on excess inventory. One week audit, then adjust ordering down 20%.

Which bucket is your biggest expense right now?

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs at The Forge

• **Pull actuals by 8am on the 1st of next month** — Don't wait. Get membership revenue (MRR breakdown: new vs. retained), class attendance rates, PT session sales, and operating costs into a spreadsheet within 24 hours of month-end. This gives you early spotting of problems.

• **Track the THREE metrics that actually move the needle for gyms:** Member churn rate (target: <5%), average revenue per member (ARPM), and cash-on-hand runway. Everything else is noise. If ARPM is dropping, you know you need more PT upsells or premium classes, not a new marketing campaign.

• **Flag one red/yellow/green action per KPI** — Don't just record numbers. Next to each metric, write what you'll DO if it's off-track. Example: "Churn 7% (RED) → launch 30-day win-back texts to lapsed members by Friday." This turns data into decisions you execute immediately.

• **Share one headline metric with your team by lunchtime** — "We added 8 members this month" or "We hit 94% utilization on peak times." People run harder when they see progress. This also keeps everyone accountable to YOUR targets.

• **Set next month's ONE priority based on this month's gaps** — Don't chase five things. If revenue per member dipped, your priority is PT conversion training. If churn spiked, it's retention calls. Update it in your calendar now so you're not improvising mid-month.

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execute Today

• **Grab last 12 months of actuals first.** Pull bank statements, membership fees, class pack sales, PT revenue, any ancillary income. You need real numbers before projections mean anything. Spend 2 hours max organizing this into a simple spreadsheet by revenue stream.

• **Model three scenarios, not one.** Build conservative (10% member churn, minimal PT growth), realistic (current trends continue), and aggressive (word-of-mouth kicks in, 15% new members). This kills false confidence and shows Grace where decision-making matters most.

• **Forecast operating costs by fixing vs. variable.** Rent, insurance, staff salaries are fixed—they barely move. Utilities, cleaning supplies, PT contractor payouts scale with members. Separate these because Grace needs to know her break-even member count and whether new growth actually hits margin.

• **Plug in the seasonality trap.** January will spike; summer will dip. Grace needs to see the cash-flow reality, not just annual averages. This determines whether she has enough runway to survive slow months without panic decisions.

• **Use this for one decision this month.** Don't let forecasting sit in a spreadsheet. "If we're only 80% occupancy in Q2, do we hire that extra instructor now or wait?" Answer it. Make forecasting a tool, not busywork.

What's the biggest revenue source right now—memberships, PT, or something else?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Mapping Profitability at The Forge

**Start with 90 days of transaction data — don't overthink it**
Pull your last quarter from your accounting system (or bank/Square records). You need: revenue per service type, direct costs (instructor pay, equipment wear, supplies), and allocated overhead (rent, utilities split proportionally). Use a basic spreadsheet — don't commission software.

**For classes: calculate per-attendee profitability, not just revenue**
A 6am spin class generating £600/month might have 8 regulars (£75/person) but a Saturday session with 20 people is £30/person. This tells you which class times *actually* make money. This drives your scheduling decisions — not attendance numbers alone.

**PT is your profit engine — measure it differently**
Separate PT revenue from class revenue immediately. PT typically has 60-70% gross margins (you get £60/hour, instructor takes £25-30). Map which PT packages sell best and which instructors have waiting lists — that's your expansion play, not necessarily more class variety.

**Add-ons reveal hidden margin**
Track protein shakes, merchandise, locker access, guest passes separately. If smoothies generate £8 per transaction at 70% margin with minimal effort, that's scaling faster than adding another class. This often shows you where upselling is broken.

**Output: one-page visual by Friday**
Three columns (classes/PT/add-ons), four rows (revenue/costs/gross margin/margin %). Share it with your top instructor or operations person — they'll spot what you've missed and validate the numbers.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Execute This Audit Like a Pro

• **Map the actual customer journey first** — Have Grace (or her ops person) manually sign up as a test enquiry through *every* channel (website form, Instagram DM, phone call, landing page) and document *exactly* what happens next. This reveals where automations actually fire vs. where they're supposed to. Takes 30 mins, saves weeks of guessing.

• **Pull the audit data from your automation tool directly** — Export all active workflows from whatever you use (Zapier, Make, native gym software). Sort by "trigger type" and look for identical rules firing on the same event (e.g., two automations both sending welcome emails on form submission). Kill duplicates immediately — they're costing money and confusing prospects with repeat messages.

• **Test the enquiry-to-trial conversion chain with fake bookings** — Create 3-5 test enquiries spaced 24 hours apart. Track: Does the welcome email arrive? Does the gym management system create a contact record? Does the calendar booking link work? Does the trial reminder go out 24 hours before? If *any* step fails, that's revenue walking out.

• **Audit for ghost automations** — Search for inactive/paused workflows you forgot about. They're technical debt. Either reactivate with clear owner or delete them to keep the system clean.

• **Document the "source of truth"** — Once audited, create a one-page flowchart showing: Enquiry → Email sequence → Trial booking → Confirmation → Reminder. Tape it to Grace's office wall. Reference it every time someone suggests a "new automation."

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge - Execution Plan

**1. Identify YOUR 3 tasks first (pick from these high-probability wins)**
- **Email inquiries** → New member questions about membership, class times, pricing (likely 10-15/week). Use Zapier + ChatGPT to auto-respond with FAQs, then flag genuine leads for you.
- **Social media engagement** → Comments on Instagram/Facebook asking "Do you do X class?" or "What's your pricing?" Buffer or MeetEdgar can template responses; ChatGPT can personalize them.
- **Cancellation follow-ups** → Members who go inactive for 2 weeks. Build a Zapier workflow that auto-sends a discount-code retention email based on your CRM data.

**2. Build the stack TODAY (no-code, 30 mins)**
- Use **Zapier** to connect your email/Instagram to ChatGPT. Set up one trigger: "New inquiry received" → ChatGPT generates response → Slack notifies you for quality check.
- Test with 5 real inquiries before full launch. Tweak the prompt to match YOUR voice/brand.

**3. Measure what matters**
- Time saved per week (track manually for 2 weeks). Target: 4+ hours freed.
- Conversion rate on auto-responses (are people booking calls/trials?). If <20%, refine the prompt—it's too generic.

**Start with email automation.** It's the easiest win and saves most time immediately.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website — Quick Execution Plan

• **Get an SSL certificate NOW (today).** If your site isn't HTTPS, you're leaking member data and tanking Google rankings. Use Cloudflare Free or your hosting provider's auto-SSL. Takes 30 mins. Cost: £0-10/year.

• **Audit what member data you're actually collecting.** Review your online booking system, payment processor, and contact forms. Document everything. This defines your actual security risk — don't over-engineer for data you don't need to store.

• **Lock down admin access immediately.** Change all passwords to 20+ character random strings. Enable 2FA on your website backend and email account. Your site admin password is the master key — treat it like that.

• **Implement one payment security layer.** If you take card payments online, ensure your processor is PCI-compliant (Stripe, Square, PayPal all are). Never store raw card data yourself. Verify this takes 15 mins of paperwork.

• **Set a 6-month security refresh.** Schedule a 30-min audit check each quarter: update plugins/software, review admin logins, test member password resets work. Stick it in your calendar now or it won't happen.

**Why this matters:** One breach loses member trust *and* could trigger GDPR fines. Members trust you with their payment info and personal schedules—that's serious liability.

What's your current booking/payment setup?
